questions.stem: A 25-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by police under Section 136. He has a 6-week history of social withdrawal, poor self-care, and worsening paranoia. He believes strangers are reading his thoughts and that his flat is bugged. He reports hearing voices commenting on his actions. On examination, he is guarded and his speech is disorganised. There is no history of mood disturbance or substance misuse. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Bipolar I disorder, current manic episode with psychotic features
questions.explanation: The presence of psychotic symptoms for longer than 1 month (6 weeks), along with negative symptoms and functional decline, meets the criteria for schizophrenia. In contrast, a manic episode would require elevated mood and increased energy; acute and transient psychotic disorder has a much shorter duration (often days to <1 month); substance-induced is excluded by no substance use; brief psychotic disorder lasts <1 month.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her husband under Section 4 (emergency detention) after expressing suicidal thoughts. Over the past month, she has felt persistently sad, lost interest in activities, and struggled with low energy and poor concentration. She wakes at 4 a.m. daily and has lost 4 kg. She has no psychotic symptoms or manic episodes. She recently lost her job but denies any substance use. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The duration (>2 weeks) and severity (multiple somatic and cognitive symptoms, weight loss, early morning waking, suicidal ideation) meet DSM-5 criteria for major depressive disorder, moderate. Adjustment disorder requires an identifiable stressor and is milder; dysthymia is chronic low-grade depression lasting >2 years; bipolar depression would require a history of mania/hypomania; prolonged grief disorder is specific to bereavement.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by police after being found shouting in the street. He claims he has special powers and is on a mission from God. For the past week, he has slept only 2 hours per night, is talkative and irritable, and has been spending large sums of money. He has no prior psychiatric history and a negative urine drug screen. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Bipolar I disorder, current manic episode with psychotic features
questions.explanation: The combination of elevated/irritable mood, decreased need for sleep, grandiosity, increased talkativeness, and goal-directed activity (overspending) for >1 week is diagnostic of a manic episode. Psychotic features (grandiose delusions) are secondary to the mood disturbance. Negative drug screen and no psychotic symptoms outside of mood episodes rule out substance-induced mania and schizophrenia; schizoaffective requires psychotic symptoms in the absence of mood episodes.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with a known history of schizophrenia on long-term haloperidol is brought to the emergency department under Section 5(2) after becoming confused and rigid. He has a temperature of 38.9°C, muscle rigidity, and fluctuating consciousness. His pulse is 110 bpm and blood pressure 160/90 mmHg. Blood tests show a raised creatine kinase. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The triad of fever, rigidity, and autonomic instability in a patient on a high-potency antipsychotic, together with raised CK, is classic for neuroleptic malignant syndrome. Serotonin syndrome typically involves gastrointestinal symptoms and clonus; malignant hyperthermia is anesthetic-related; catatonia may present with rigidity but not fever; encephalitis would show CSF pleocytosis.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her husband under Section 136, 2 weeks after the birth of her first child. She has been acting strangely: not sleeping, claiming the baby is an alien, and laughing inappropriately. On examination, she is disorganised and appears to be responding to unseen stimuli. She has no previous psychiatric history. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Bipolar I disorder, first manic episode
questions.explanation: Postpartum psychosis typically presents within weeks of delivery with rapid onset of psychotic symptoms, mood instability, and confusion. The timeframe and severity point to postpartum psychosis rather than postpartum depression (which lacks psychosis). A first manic episode could appear similar, but postpartum psychosis is a recognised distinct entity often considered as a bipolar spectrum disorder. Brief psychotic disorder lasts <1 month but would not account for the postpartum context specificity.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman is brought to the Emergency Department by police under Section 136 of the Mental Health Act after she was found shouting on a bridge. She is agitated, thought-disordered, and refuses to speak. She has no prior psychiatric history. What is the most appropriate next step to assess whether she meets criteria for further detention?
options: Apply for a Section 2 admission for assessment
Apply for a Section 3 admission for treatment
Request an assessment by an approved mental health professional (AMHP) and two doctors
questions.explanation: Section 136 allows detention for up to 24 hours for assessment. The next step is a formal MHA assessment by an AMHP and two doctors (one Section 12 approved) to decide if Section 2 or 3 is warranted. Applying for Section 2/3 without assessment is premature, medication is not a legal step, and discharge is unsafe given her acute state.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man on a medical ward for pneumonia has been refusing treatment and attempting to leave. He has a history of bipolar disorder and is known to be manic. He is not formally detained. The nurse calls you because he is becoming aggressive. What is the most appropriate immediate action to prevent him from leaving while a formal MHA assessment is arranged?
options: Detain him under Section 5(2) of the MHA
Use physical restraint to keep him in bed
Detain him under Section 5(4) of the MHA
questions.explanation: Section 5(2) allows the responsible clinician or nominated deputy to detain an informal inpatient for up to 72 hours pending a full MHA assessment. Physical restraint without legal authority is inappropriate; lorazepam may be used to manage agitation but does not provide legal detention; Section 5(4) is for nurses (limited to 6 hours).

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman with dementia is brought to hospital by her son because she has become increasingly confused and aggressive. She is unable to give consent. The clinical team suspects a urinary tract infection. Blood tests are pending. She wants to go home. What is the most appropriate criterion to determine whether she can be detained under the Mental Health Act?
options: Presence of a mental disorder of a nature or degree that warrants detention
Inability to make decisions about admission
Need for involuntary treatment for physical health
Lack of capacity to consent to admission
Risk of harm to self that is immediate
questions.explanation: The MHA requires a mental disorder (dementia qualifies) and that detention is necessary for the health or safety of the patient or protection of others. Inability to consent (capacity) is managed under the Mental Capacity Act, not MHA; immediate risk is too narrow; physical health needs alone do not justify MHA detention.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man known with schizophrenia is found by the police in a distressed state outside a supermarket. He is paranoid, refuses to engage, and has disorganised speech. He has been non-adherent with medication for weeks. There is no immediate risk of violence but he is neglecting himself. The police have placed him under Section 136 and brought him to a place of safety (a hospital). What is the most appropriate next investigation to decide if he can be detained for treatment?
options: Full blood count and inflammatory markers
Assessment by AMHP and two doctors
questions.explanation: The decision for detention under MHA rests on a formal assessment by an AMHP and two doctors (one Section 12 approved). Urine toxicology is helpful but not definitive; bloods and CT exclude organic causes but are secondary; MMSE is not diagnostic for detention criteria.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with borderline personality disorder is brought to the Emergency Department by police under Section 136 after a suicide attempt (overdose). She has been medically cleared. She now states she wants to leave. She has no psychotic symptoms. What is the most important criterion to justify continued detention under the Mental Health Act?
options: She is at immediate risk of suicide
She has capacity to refuse treatment
She has a history of previous admissions
questions.explanation: The MHA permits detention if it is necessary for the health or safety of the patient (immediate suicide risk) or protection of others. Simply having a mental disorder (including personality disorder) is insufficient; capacity to refuse treatment would support voluntary status; history alone does not justify detention.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with known bipolar I disorder presents to the emergency department with acute mania (agitation, pressure of speech, grandiose delusions). He refuses all medication and is deemed to lack capacity. He is detained under Section 4 of the Mental Health Act. What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological treatment for his acute manic episode?
questions.explanation: For acute mania requiring rapid tranquillisation in a detained patient, intramuscular olanzapine is first-line due to its rapid onset and favourable side-effect profile. Haloperidol, though effective, carries a higher risk of extrapyramidal side effects. Lorazepam alone is insufficient for mania. Lithium and valproate are maintenance therapies, not appropriate for acute management.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with a history of borderline personality disorder is brought to the emergency department under Section 136 after self-harm (superficial cuts) and suicidal ideation. She is calmer but still distressed. She is assessed by the psychiatric liaison team and deemed to have capacity. What is the most appropriate management for her immediate emotional distress?
options: Detain under Section 2 for treatment with risperidone
Offer psychological support and a low-stimulus environment
questions.explanation: In borderline personality disorder, the first-line management for acute distress is psychological support and a calm environment, not medication. Pharmacotherapy is not indicated for brief emotional crises. Benzodiazepines may worsen impulsivity, and antipsychotics have no proven benefit for self-limiting distress.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with Alzheimer's disease is detained under Section 2 after becoming severely agitated and aggressive towards his wife. He has no other medical history. He has not responded to verbal de-escalation. What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological treatment for his agitation?
questions.explanation: In elderly patients with dementia and severe agitation requiring rapid tranquillisation, low-dose haloperidol (0.5–1 mg) is first-line due to its potency and low anticholinergic burden. Olanzapine increases stroke risk and is not recommended. Lorazepam may cause paradoxical disinhibition. Risperidone and trazodone are slower acting.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman with first-episode psychosis (paranoid delusions, auditory hallucinations) is brought to hospital under Section 4 after being found wandering in the street. She is distressed and refusing to eat. She is not aggressive. She has no prior medical history and is not pregnant. What is the most appropriate antipsychotic for her initial treatment?
questions.explanation: For first-episode psychosis, the first-line antipsychotic is a second-generation agent with a favourable side-effect profile; aripiprazole or olanzapine are appropriate. However, olanzapine is associated with significant weight gain and metabolic effects, so aripiprazole is often preferred as first-line. Clozapine is reserved for treatment-resistant psychosis. Haloperidol intramuscularly is for rapid tranquillisation, not needed here. Chlorpromazine is older and has more side effects.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old man with schizophrenia, well controlled on clozapine 300 mg daily, presents with acute agitation and paranoid delusions after stopping his medication for 2 weeks. He is detained under Section 3. He has a history of neutropenia (ANC 1.2 x10^9/L) but is currently stable. What is the most appropriate immediate pharmacological management?
options: Restart clozapine at 300 mg daily immediately
questions.explanation: Since the patient has previously responded well to clozapine but stopped it, the optimal strategy is to restart clozapine, but not immediately due to the risk of neutropenia (ANC borderline). Aripiprazole is a reasonable alternative as a second-generation antipsychotic with lower haematological risk. Haloperidol and lorazepam are for acute sedation, not definitive treatment. Olanzapine is an option but may delay clozapine restart.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man is brought to ED by police under Section 136 after being found shouting on a street, apparently responding to internal stimuli. He is unkempt and uncooperative. On arrival, he is calm but refuses to speak. No physical abnormalities are obvious. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer him to the psychiatric liaison team
Assess his capacity to consent to admission
Apply for a Section 2 detention assessment
questions.explanation: In a patient with new psychotic symptoms, the first step is to exclude an organic cause. A thorough physical examination is essential before any psychiatric detention or treatment. Capacity assessment can follow once medical causes are ruled out.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with known bipolar disorder is brought to ED by her husband. She is elated, disinhibited, and grandiose, but not violent. She refuses admission, stating she is fine. She has poor insight. Observations are normal. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Discuss with the on-call consultant psychiatrist
Assess her capacity to refuse treatment
Detain her under Section 5(2) of the MHA
questions.explanation: Before using the Mental Health Act, you must first determine if the patient lacks capacity to make this decision. If she lacks capacity and is at risk, then further detention under MHA can be considered. The most tempting distractor is discussing with a consultant, but capacity assessment is the immediate priority.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man is admitted informally with first-episode psychosis. He suddenly becomes agitated, threatening to leave and harm other patients. He has not taken any medication. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Use Section 5(2) of the MHA to detain him
Call security to physically restrain him
questions.explanation: In an acutely agitated patient, verbal de-escalation is the first-line intervention. If it fails, then pharmacological sedation or using MHA holding powers may be indicated. The most tempting distractor is Section 5(2), but de-escalation should be attempted first.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man is brought to ED by ambulance after a paracetamol overdose 2 hours ago. He is alert and oriented, but says he is suicidal and wants to leave. He refuses any treatment. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Assess his mental capacity to refuse treatment
Detain him under Section 4 of the MHA
Arrange a psychiatric assessment for Section 2
Refer him to the crisis team for outpatient follow-up
questions.explanation: The immediate priority is to treat the life-threatening paracetamol overdose. N-acetylcysteine is effective within 8 hours and cannot be delayed. Capacity assessment and MHA detention can occur after medical treatment has commenced.

questions.stem: An 18-year-old woman with borderline personality disorder presents to ED after cutting her forearms superficially. She is calm, but states she will self-harm again if sent home. She has capacity and is not currently suicidal. She has had multiple similar admissions. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Discharge her with a crisis plan
Offer a short admission for respite
Detain her under Section 2 of the MHA
Refer her to the liaison psychiatry team
Prescribe a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor
questions.explanation: For patients with borderline personality disorder, brief admissions often reinforce self-harm behaviour. Liaison psychiatry can provide a comprehensive assessment and formulate a management plan. Detention under MHA is not indicated when capacity is present and risk is not imminent.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old university student is brought to the emergency department by police after being found shouting incoherently in the street, appearing dishevelled and responding to unseen stimuli. On mental state examination, he is suspicious and discloses that voices are telling him to 'end it all'. Which single finding most clearly indicates that this patient meets the threshold for emergency detention under the Mental Health Act?
questions.explanation: Command hallucinations to harm himself represent an immediate risk of serious self-harm, which meets the 'risk to self' criterion for emergency detention under Section 4 or 136. The other options are concerning but do not alone indicate imminent danger.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with known bipolar disorder presents to his GP with elevated mood, pressured speech, and grandiose plans. His wife reports he has been sleeping only 3 hours per night and has started spending large sums of money. He refuses medication and any form of help. Which single feature, if present, would indicate that his condition has escalated to a psychiatric emergency requiring immediate assessment under the Mental Health Act?
options: Threats to harm his wife with a knife he has purchased
questions.explanation: Threats to harm his wife with a knife indicate a clear and imminent risk to others, which is the key threshold for emergency detention. Irritability or poor compliance alone do not meet this threshold.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with paranoid schizophrenia is found by police barricaded in his flat, shouting that people are trying to kill him. He is holding a large kitchen knife and has barricaded the door. Which single finding most strongly supports the need for immediate detention under the Mental Health Act on the grounds of risk to others?
options: Possession of a weapon and threatening behaviour
questions.explanation: Possession of a weapon combined with threatening behaviour represents an imminent threat of serious harm to others, fulfilling Section 136 or 4 criteria. Past history is relevant but not current; hallucinations and delusions alone do not indicate imminent danger.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman with dementia develops acute confusion on a medical ward during treatment for a urinary tract infection. She is agitated, trying to pull out her IV line, and yelling that 'they are poisoning her'. The nursing staff request a psychiatric assessment for possible detention. Which single clinical finding suggests that this is likely a delirium (organic cause) rather than a functional psychiatric disorder, and that emergency detention under the Mental Health Act may be inappropriate?
questions.explanation: Fluctuating consciousness with reduced attention is the hallmark of delirium. While other features overlap with functional disorders, the presence of clouded consciousness points strongly to an organic cause, and treatment of the underlying infection takes priority over psychiatric detention.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old girl with anorexia nervosa is brought to the emergency department by her parents, who report she has refused all food and fluids for the past 3 days. Her weight is 35 kg (BMI 12.5), and she is visibly cachectic. Which single finding most clearly indicates that there is an immediate risk to life justifying emergency detention under the Mental Health Act for treatment of her eating disorder?
options: Expressed wish to lose more weight
Electrocardiogram showing prolonged QTc interval of 520 ms
questions.explanation: A prolonged QTc interval (> 500 ms) indicates a high risk of fatal cardiac arrhythmia, representing an immediate life-threatening complication of anorexia. While BMI < 13 is very low, it is the cardiac risk that constitutes an emergency requiring detention to prevent imminent death.
